--- SOURCE 1: Computational astrophysics - Wikipedia ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computational_astrophysics

SUMMARY:
Important techniques of computational astrophysics include particle-in-cell (PIC) and the closely related particle-mesh (PM), N-body simulations, Monte Carlo

FULL CONTENT:
![](https://en.wikipedia.org/static/images/donate/donate.gif)
![Wikipedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-wordmark-en.svg)

# Computational astrophysics

**Computational astrophysics** refers to the methods and computing tools developed and used in [astrophysics](/wiki/Astrophysics "Astrophysics") research. Like [computational chemistry](/wiki/Computational_chemistry "Computational chemistry") or [computational physics](/wiki/Computational_physics "Computational physics"), it is both a specific branch of [theoretical astrophysics](/wiki/Theoretical_astrophysics "Theoretical astrophysics") and an interdisciplinary field relying on [computer science](/wiki/Computer_science "Computer science"), [mathematics](/wiki/Mathematics "Mathematics"), and wider [physics](/wiki/Physics "Physics"). Computational astrophysics is most often studied through an applied mathematics or astrophysics programme at PhD level.

Well-established areas of astrophysics employing computational methods include [magnetohydrodynamics](/wiki/Magnetohydrodynamics#Astrophysics "Magnetohydrodynamics"), astrophysical radiative transfer, [stellar](/wiki/Stellar_dynamics "Stellar dynamics") and [galactic](/wiki/Galaxy "Galaxy") dynamics, and astrophysical [fluid dynamics](/wiki/Fluid_dynamics "Fluid dynamics"). A recently developed field with interesting results is [numerical relativity](/wiki/Numerical_relativity "Numerical relativity").

## Contents

## Research

Many astrophysicists use computers in their work, and a growing number of astrophysics departments now have research groups specially devoted to computational astrophysics. Important research initiatives include the [US Department of Energy](/wiki/US_Department_of_Energy "US Department of Energy") (DoE) [SciDAC](/wiki/Energy_Citations_Database#SciDAC "Energy Citations Database") collaboration for astrophysics[[1]](#cite_note-SciDAC-1) and the now defunct European AstroSim collaboration.[[2]](#cite_note-2) A notable active project is the international [Virgo Consortium](/wiki/Virgo_Consortium "Virgo Consortium"), which focuses on cosmology.

In August 2015 during the general assembly of the International Astronomical Union a new
[commission C.B1 on Computational Astrophysics](https://web.archive.org/web/20150912041337/http://www.iau.org/submissions/newcommissions/detail/62/) was inaugurated, therewith recognizing the importance of astronomical discovery by computing.

Important techniques of computational astrophysics include [particle-in-cell](/wiki/Particle-in-cell "Particle-in-cell") (PIC) and the closely related [particle-mesh](/wiki/Particle-mesh "Particle-mesh") (PM), [N-body simulations](/wiki/N-body_simulations "N-body simulations"), [Monte Carlo methods](/wiki/Monte_Carlo_methods "Monte Carlo methods"), as well as [grid-free](/wiki/Meshfree_methods "Meshfree methods") (with [smoothed particle hydrodynamics](/wiki/Smoothed_particle_hydrodynamics "Smoothed particle hydrodynamics") (SPH) being an important example) and [grid-based](/wiki/Regular_grid "Regular grid") methods for fluids. In addition, methods from [numerical analysis](/wiki/Numerical_analysis "Numerical analysis") for solving [ODEs](/wiki/Ordinary_differential_equations "Ordinary differential equations") and [PDEs](/wiki/Partial_differential_equations "Partial differential equations") are also used.

[Simulation](/wiki/Simulation "Simulation") of astrophysical flows is of particular importance as many objects and processes of astronomical interest such as stars and nebulae involve gases. [Fluid computer models](/wiki/Computational_Fluid_Dynamics "Computational Fluid Dynamics") are often coupled with radiative transfer, (Newtonian) gravity, nuclear physics and (general) relativity to study highly energetic phenomena such as supernovae, [relativistic jets](/wiki/Relativistic_jets "Relativistic jets"), [active galaxies](/wiki/Active_galaxies "Active galaxies") and [gamma-ray bursts](/wiki/Gamma-ray_bursts "Gamma-ray bursts")[[3]](#cite_note-3) and are also used to model [stellar structure](/wiki/Stellar_structure "Stellar structure"), [planetary formation](/wiki/Planetary_formation "Planetary formation"), [evolution of stars](/wiki/Stellar_evolution "Stellar evolution") and [of galaxies](/wiki/Galactic_evolution "Galactic evolution"), and exotic objects such as [neutron stars](/wiki/Neutron_stars "Neutron stars"), [pulsars](/wiki/Pulsars "Pulsars"), [magnetars](/wiki/Magnetars "Magnetars") and black holes.[[4]](#cite_note-4) [Computer simulations](/wiki/Computer_simulation "Computer simulation") are often the only means to study [stellar collisions](/wiki/Stellar_collisions "Stellar collisions"), [galaxy mergers](/wiki/Galaxy_merger "Galaxy merger"), as well as [galactic](/wiki/Interacting_galaxy "Interacting galaxy") and black hole interactions.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

In recent years the field has made increasing use of [parallel](/wiki/Parallel_computing "Parallel computing") and [high performance computers](/wiki/Supercomputer "Supercomputer").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Tools

Computational astrophysics as a field makes extensive use of software and hardware technologies. These systems are often highly specialized and made by dedicated professionals, and so generally find limited popularity in the wider (computational) physics community.

### Hardware

Like other similar fields, computational astrophysics makes extensive use of supercomputers and [computer clusters](/wiki/Computer_clusters "Computer clusters") . Even on the scale of a normal desktop it is possible to [accelerate the hardware](/wiki/Hardware_accelerator "Hardware accelerator"). Perhaps the most notable such [computer architecture](/wiki/Computer_architecture "Computer architecture") built specially for astrophysics is the [GRAPE (gravity pipe)](/wiki/Gravity_Pipe "Gravity Pipe") in Japan.

As of 2010, the biggest N-body simulations, such as [DEGIMA](/wiki/DEGIMA "DEGIMA"), do [general-purpose computing on graphics processing units](/wiki/General-purpose_computing_on_graphics_processing_units "General-purpose computing on graphics processing units").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

### Software

Many codes and software packages, exist along with various researchers and consortia maintaining them. Most codes tend to be
n-body packages or fluid solvers of some sort. Examples of n-body codes include [ChaNGa](/wiki/ChaNGa "ChaNGa"), MODEST,[[9]](#cite_note-9) nbodylab.org[[10]](#cite_note-10) and Starlab.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

For hydrodynamics there is usually a coupling between codes, as the motion of the fluids usually has some other effect (such as gravity, or radiation) in astrophysical situations. For example, for SPH/N-body there is [GADGET](/wiki/GADGET "GADGET") and SWIFT;[[12]](#cite_note-12) for grid-based/N-body RAMSES,[[13]](#cite_note-13) ENZO,[[14]](#cite_note-14) FLASH,[[15]](#cite_note-15) and ART.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

AMUSE [[2]](http://amusecode.org/),[[17]](#cite_note-17) takes a different approach (called Noah's Ark[[18]](#cite_note-18)) than the other packages by providing an interface structure to a large number of publicly available astronomical codes for addressing stellar dynamics, stellar evolution, hydrodynamics and radiative transport.

# Computational Astrophysics

![](/content/michigan-lsa/astro/en/research/computational-astrophysics/jcr:content/par/image.transform/xbigfree/image.jpg)

Computational Astrophysics is a rapidly growing branch of astrophysical research. Computer models simulate the fundamental physical processes at work in distant astronomical objects, seeking to accurately account for new observational data coming from large-scale surveys and precision observations. Researchers in the department develop novel numerical methods, including N-body codes, gravity and hydrodynamic schemes, radiative transfer codes, chemical networks, and large-volume data mining techniques, to model:

*Image Credit: NASA/AEI/ZIB/M. Koppitz & L. Rezzolla*

FULL CONTENT:
# Center for Computational Astrophysics

# [Featured News](https://www.simonsfoundation.org/flatiron/center-for-computational-astrophysics/news)

![A visualization shows a giant flare erupting from a magnetized star called a magnetar.](https://simonsfoundation.imgix.net/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/21105733/FI_Lede_Magnetar_Nasa_JPL-Caltech_1180x660.jpg?auto=format&w=581&h=330&fit=crop&q=90)

Flatiron Institute researchers have identified a previously unknown way to make the universe’s heaviest atoms, such as gold, platinum and uranium. They calculate that a single flare from a supermagnetized star called a magnetar can produce the mass equivalent of 27 moons’ worth of these elements in one go.

The Center for Computational Astrophysics executes research programs on systems ranging in scales from planets to cosmology, creating and using computational tools for data analysis and theory. It also supports, trains, and equips diverse members of the global astrophysics community and convenes events and workshops in New York City.

Our Mission:  
▪ Solve important, hard problems in computational astrophysics. Focus on problems that we at Flatiron are uniquely positioned to solve. ▪ Invent and propagate better data-analysis practices, analytical methods and computational methods for the global astrophysics community, with a focus on rigor. ▪ Develop, maintain and contribute to open-source software packages, open data and their communities. ▪ Create and support a community of astrophysics doers, learners and mentors in New York City and beyond. ▪ Train and launch diverse early-career researchers in astrophysics with unique capabilities in computational methods.

### Astrometry.net

If you have astronomical imaging of the sky with celestial coordinates you do not know—or do not trust—then Astrometry.net is for you. Input an image and we'll give you back astrometric calibration meta-data, plus lists of known objects falling inside the field of view.

If you have astronomical imaging of the sky with celestial coordinates you do not know—or do not trust—then Astrometry.net is for you. Input an image and we'll give you back astrometric calibration meta-data, plus lists of known objects falling inside the field of view.

### celerite

celerite is a library for fast and scalable Gaussian Process (GP) Regression in one dimension with implementations in C++, Python, and Julia.

celerite is a library for fast and scalable Gaussian Process (GP) Regression in one dimension with implementations in C++, Python, and Julia.

### DAFT

Daft is a Python package that uses matplotlib to render pixel-perfect probabilistic graphical models for publication in a journal or on the internet.

Daft is a Python package that uses matplotlib to render pixel-perfect probabilistic graphical models for publication in a journal or on the internet.

### emcee

emcee is an extensible, pure-Python implementation of Goodman & Weare's Affine Invariant Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) Ensemble sampler. It's designed for Bayesian parameter estimation.

emcee is an extensible, pure-Python implementation of Goodman & Weare's Affine Invariant Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) Ensemble sampler. It's designed for Bayesian parameter estimation.

![](https://simonsfoundation.imgix.net/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/01101902/Exp_CCA_800x400.png?auto=format&w=400&h=200&fit=crop&q=90)

### EXP

The EXP C++ library is an efficient N-body simulation toolkit that implements basis-function methods using hybrid CPU and GPU code alongside Python bindings.

The EXP C++ library is an efficient N-body simulation toolkit that implements basis-function methods using hybrid CPU and GPU code alongside Python bindings.

![](https://simonsfoundation.imgix.net/wp-content/uploads/2022/07/27120531/Gala_800x400.jpg?auto=format&w=400&h=200&fit=crop&q=90)

### Gala

Galactic Dynamics is the study of the formation, history, and evolution of galaxies using the orbits of objects — numerically-integrated trajectories of stars, dark matter particles, star clusters, or galaxies themselves.

Galactic Dynamics is the study of the formation, history, and evolution of galaxies using the orbits of objects — numerically-integrated trajectories of stars, dark matter particles, star clusters, or galaxies themselves.

![](https://simonsfoundation.imgix.net/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/13161725/Flatiron-Institute-CCA%402x.png?auto=format&w=400&h=200&fit=crop&q=90)

### George

George is a fast and flexible Python library for Gaussian Process Regression. It capitalizes on the Hierarchical Off-Diagonal Low-Rank formalism to make controlled approximations for fast execution.

George is a fast and flexible Python library for Gaussian Process Regression. It capitalizes on the Hierarchical Off-Diagonal Low-Rank formalism to make controlled approximations for fast execution.

### MESA

MESA is a robust suite of open-source, robust, efficient, thread-safe libraries extensively used in computational stellar astrophysics.

### STARRY

starry enables the computation of fast and precise light curves for various applications in astronomy: transits and secondary eclipses of exoplanets, light curves of eclipsing binaries, rotational phase curves of exoplanets, light curves of planet-planet and planet-moon occultations, and more.

Simulating the three-dimensional structure of magnetic fields and materials around a newborn star. · Modeling spiral galaxies to understand how their spiral arms

FULL CONTENT:
## Main navigation

## Utility Menu

# Computational Astrophysics

## Breadcrumb

Theory is how scientists fit data collected from observation into a system of understanding. However, some astrophysical systems are too complicated to be treated adequately using straightforward theoretical calculations. These include the extreme environments near stars, black holes, and other places where strong gravity, magnetic fields, and high-temperature matter combine in complex ways. Systems like those require computational astrophysics, where researchers simulate the system on a computer and compare what they find with observations.

## Our Work

Center for Astrophysics | Harvard & Smithsonian astrophysicists use computer models for a huge variety of astronomical systems:

Simulating the three-dimensional structure of magnetic fields and materials around a newborn star. The role of magnetic fields in star formation is mysterious, so researchers compare astronomical observations to the results from computational simulations to understand what’s going on.  
[Chaotically Magnetized Cloud Is No Place to Build a Star, or Is It?](/news/chaotically-magnetized-cloud-no-place-build-star-or-it)

Modeling spiral galaxies to understand how their spiral arms form. Observations alone can’t tell us how stable spiral arms are over long periods of time, so theoretical astrophysicists use computational models to test plausible explanations for their origin and stability.  
[New Insights on How Spiral Galaxies Get Their Arms](/news/new-insights-how-spiral-galaxies-get-their-arms)

Simulating the formation of galaxies on cosmic scales, to model the large-scale structure of the universe. The Illustris Project is a powerful computer program that simulates galaxy formation and merging using models of dark matter and gas particles. This simulation produces a realistic approximation of a part of the cosmic web of galaxies as it evolves over billions of years.  
[The Cosmic Evolution of Galaxies](/news/cosmic-evolution-galaxies)

## The Cosmos Via Computer

Over the past few decades, computers have become fast enough and storage cheap enough to make computational astrophysics a powerful tool for astronomy, alongside telescope or spacecraft observations, theoretical models, and [laboratory experiments](/research/science-field/laboratory-astrophysics). Every astronomical researcher today uses computers in some aspect of their work, but computational astrophysics — creating computer models of entire astrophysical systems — is an established method on its own, providing a way to tackle problems that would be otherwise too time-consuming or difficult.

Computational astrophysics is complementary to both lab work and theory, allowing researchers to run many simulations of the same system, or change the parameters of an astrophysical model to check how the outcomes differ. Computation can also be used to compare different models of a system to determine which one fits observational data best.

Computational astrophysics is an important method in many different areas of research:

Extreme environments demand computational astrophysics. These include star-forming regions, the environments surrounding neutron stars or black holes, and other places where gravity, magnetism, and high temperatures rule the behavior of matter. Known as magnetohydrodynamics, this research is some of the most computationally challenging but important work in theoretical astrophysics. [ link to “jets shocks & outflows” page ]

Galaxies are remarkably complex systems, involving interactions between stars, gas, dust, and the invisible [dark matter](/research/topic/dark-energy-and-dark-matter) holding everything together. Astrophysicists studying galactic dynamics model the disks, spiral arms, and other features of galaxies, to compare with observations. Other research simulates galaxy collisions and galaxy formation. [ link to “galaxy formation and evolution” page ]

On an even grander level, researchers compute how galaxies form and organize on cosmic scales. These simulations of the [large-scale structure](/research/topic/large-scale-structure) of the universe help us understand the “cosmic web” and voids that astronomers see in galaxy surveys.

More mundanely, computational astrophysics is necessary when processing massive datasets from telescopes. “Big data” requires computers to process, including [new statistical algorithms](/research/topic/astrostatistics) and [machine learning](/research/topic/machine-learning) to extract meaning from the sheer amount of raw data produced by modern observatories.

![simulation of the effect of primordial light scattering from hot gas in galaxy clusters](/sites/default/files/styles/max_650x650/public/2019-04/ComputationalAstroyphysics_2.jpg?itok=via-zCao)

A simulation using the Illustris project of the effect of light from the cosmic microwave background scattering from hot gas in galaxy clusters. This is known as the Sunyaev-Zel'dovich effect, which allows researchers to obtain important information about the evolution of the cosmos.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Computational astrophysics employs methods like N-body simulations and Monte Carlo techniques to model complex astrophysical phenomena, enabling researchers to analyze systems that are otherwise intractable.
Applications span from simulating galaxy formation to modeling stellar evolution and dynamics, providing insights into cosmic structures and processes, enhancing our understanding of the universe.

enriched_points.point_title: Address Current Challenges in the Field
enriched_points.expanded_content: Challenges include managing massive datasets, ensuring computational accuracy, and developing efficient algorithms for parallel computing, which are essential for advancing astrophysical research and simulations.
